segments.s374-g41: پس is an adverb meaning “then” (introductory time/sequence). اندر نهادند is a classical/poetic verb phrase: نهادند is past tense, 3rd person plural “they placed/put/laid into” and here, with اندر, functions idiomatically as “fell into / set upon / attacked.” ایرانیان is the subject in the plural (“the Iranians”). Word order is subject after the verb phrase for stylistic/poetic effect.
segments.s374-g42: بدان is the prepositional/demonstrative cluster (به + آن) meaning “to/at/that,” here introducing the object targeted by the attack. بی‌مره is an adjectival phrase “without mercy / merciless,” modifying لشکر. لشکر چینیان is the noun phrase “the host/army of the Chinese” (لشکر = host/army; چینیان = the Chinese). The whole segment provides the object of اندر نهادند.
segments.s374-g43: بکشتند is past tense, 3rd person plural ‘they killed.’ زیشان is the contracted sequence زِ + ایشان (زِ/ازِ meaning “of/from” + ایشان “them”), so it means “of them / from them” (i.e. “many of them were killed”). به هر سو is a locative/directional phrase “toward every side / in every direction.” بسی is an adverb meaning “much / a great deal,” qualifying the extent of killing.
segments.s374-g44: نبخشودشان is past tense negative, 3rd person plural with enclitic object شان: “they did not spare them / spared them not.” ای شگفتی is an inserted exclamation “O wonder / what a marvel,” parenthetical and emotive. کسی here, following the negative verb, yields the sense ‘no one / nobody’ (literally ‘anyone’ but negated to mean none). Together the clause states that nobody was spared, with the exclamation inserted for emphasis.
segments.s374-g33: همی is an iterative/progressive adverb placed before the verb to give a continuous or habitual sense. دوختشان is دوخت (past 'sewed') with the enclitic شان 'them' attached; in this poetic word order the verb comes before its objects. سینه‌ها is the first direct object ('chests'), and باز پشت is a second object/phrase where باز (here 'open') qualifies پشت ('backs').
segments.s374-g34: چنان تا is a result/consequence connector ('so that'); تا introduces the result clause. همه is a quantifier modifying سرکشان, and را is the definite/object marker attached after the NP. بکشت is an archaic/persuasive past form (with the prefixed b-) serving as the main past verb 'killed' that closes the clause.
segments.s374-g35: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. دانست is the simple past 'knew/learned' with خاقان as subject. The clause continues with a content clause introduced by که; ماندند is past plural 'remained/was left' and بس functions as a predicate/adverbial 'enough' placed after the verb in classical word order.
segments.s374-g36: This line uses an archaic/poetic construction: نیارد is an old/irregular verb-form here (contextually 'did not/was not to be') followed by شدن (infinitive/auxiliary 'to become/come') and the adverbial/prepositional phrase پیش او ('before him'). هیچ‌کس is the indefinite subject/object 'no one'. Together the phrase reads idiomatically as 'no one came forward before him.'
segments.s374-g37: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. جنب جنبان is a reduplicated verbal phrase meaning 'to move / to set stirring'; شد is the past of شدن giving an inchoative sense 'came to be/was set in motion.' و links the coordinated clause کار گشت, where کار ('the affair') is the subject and گشت is the past 'became/turned'.
segments.s374-g38: همی بود is a copular/imperfect phrase 'it was thus/continued so' with همی adding a continuing sense. تا introduces the temporal clause 'until', and گذشت is the past of گذشتن 'passed'; اندر روز places the time 'during the day.' The whole segment means the state persisted until the day passed.
segments.s374-g39: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately/at that moment'. اندر is a locative preposition 'in/into', while گریغ is a nominal predicate meaning 'sulking/resentful/haughty mood'. اوفتاد is the past intransitive 'fell' (literally 'it befell him' or 'he fell into'), so the phrase means 'he fell into a sullen/angry state.'
segments.s374-g40: بشد here means 'then it happened/so he did' (a narrative inceptive). رویش is روی + the 3rd‑person possessive -ش 'his face/turn'; اندر بیابان is the prepositional phrase 'into the desert/wilderness', and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' used idiomatically with رویش to mean 'he turned his face toward' or 'he set off into' the desert. The verb completes the action begun by بشد.
segments.s374-g9: سیم/بهره here is a personal name (compound Sim‑Bahre) and بهره is used as a name rather than its ordinary noun meaning. را is the classical direct‑object marker attaching to بهره. سوی خود is a prepositional phrase 'toward himself / to himself' (سوی = toward, خود = himself). بازداشت is a past tense 3rd‑person form (from بازداشتن) meaning 'kept/held back' (i.e., 'took/kept to himself').
segments.s374-g10: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause ('for/because'). چون here means 'as/like'. ابر غرنده آواز is a simile: ابر 'cloud', غرنده 'roaring' (adjectival), آواز 'voice'. داشت is the past of داشتن 'had' (possession)—'he had (a) roaring voice'.
segments.s374-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بستور is an adverb 'in such a manner / thus'. فرخنده و پاک are coordinate adjectives 'auspicious/fortunate and pure', and تن ('body') describes the person as 'of pure body' or 'pure‑bodied' (an epithet).
segments.s374-g12: دگر functions as 'another' (another person). فرش here means 'ground/earth' (rather than carpet) and is the direct object. آورد is the past 3rd‑person 'brought/caused'. شمشیر زن is a compound meaning 'swordsman' or 'one who strikes with the sword'—زن is the agentive/verb sense 'to strike', not 'woman'. Note the older word order: the agent (شمشیر زن) follows the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s374-g13: بهم here means 'together / in a mass' (they formed up together). ایستادند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they stood' or 'they halted'. از پیش اوی is a locative/relative phrase 'in front of him' or 'before him'—از + پیش = 'from/before', and اوی is the archaic enclitic third‑person pronoun 'him'.
segments.s374-g14: که introduces the reason/explanatory clause ('for / because'). لشکر شکستن is a noun/verb phrase using the infinitive/masdar 'breaking (of the) army' = 'rout/defeat of an army'. بدی modifies کیش: بدی کیش = 'an evil/customary tendency' or 'bad habit/character'. اوی is the possessive/enclitic 'his'—so the phrase means 'for the breaking of armies was his bad habit/nature.'
segments.s374-g15: همیدون is a proper name (the subject). ببستند is past 3rd‑person plural of بستن with the sense 'they concluded/they bound' (here 'they made/they swore'). پیمان is 'a pact/treaty/pledge'. برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این = 'upon this / on this matter', linking the pact to the following conditional clause.
segments.s374-g16: که‌گر (که + گر) is a conditional 'that if' (i.e., 'that if/should'). تیغ دشمن is a genitive phrase 'the enemy's sword' (تیغ = sword, دشمن = enemy). بدرد here is a classical verbal form meaning 'should strike / should wound / should reach' (a hypothetical/subjunctive sense following the conditional), and زمین is 'the ground/earth' (the thing struck). The clause sets a conditional consequence: 'if the enemy's blade should strike the ground...'.
segments.s374-g25: ازیشان is the fused form of از + ایشان (= 'of/from them') placed before the verb; بکشتند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (they killed) with the archaic prefix ب- preserved in epic style. چندان is an intensifying quantifier ('so much/so many') that precedes the noun سپاه ('an army, host'), so the phrase means 'they killed so many (of them) — an army (worth)'.
segments.s374-g26: کزان = 'از که/از آن' ('from which' / 'so that') introduces the resultant clause; تنگ شد is a two-word predicate meaning 'became narrow' (شد, past 'became', follows the adjective تنگ). جای آوردگاه = 'the place/space of the battlefield' (جای = place/space modifying آوردگاه = battlefield). Together: 'from which the battlefield's space became narrow.'
segments.s374-g27: چنان is an intensifier ('so/such'); خون is the theme 'blood' (here physical blood/bloodshed). همی + رفت forms an archaic progressive/past (همی marking continuous aspect, رفت = 'went/flowed'), so همی رفت = 'kept flowing' or 'flowed on.' بر governs 'کوه و دشت' ('over/on mountain and plain') as the location of the flowing blood.
segments.s374-g28: کزان ('from which/so that') links this result to the preceding clause; آسیاها here is 'the mills/millstones' (آسیا plural) rather than the continent; به + خون indicates the state/result 'in blood' or 'stained with blood'; بربگشت is a past form 'turned back/returned' (here 'were turned/covered'). The clause says the mills were turned back (i.e., became stained) with blood.
segments.s374-g29: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). ارجاسپ is the personal name (subject). آن ('that') refers back to the foregoing event; دید is simple past 'saw.' کامدش is an archaic past form meaning 'came/advanced' (کامد) with the enclitic (here included in the token); پیش = 'before/in the presence of' marking to whom he advanced.
segments.s374-g30: ابا is problematic in this line (an archaic/variant reading); none of the printed dictionary senses ('to refuse') fits straightforwardly, so here it functions as an archaic particle/verb-form introducing or addressing the group that follows. نامداران و مردان = 'the renowned (champions) and the men' (نامداران = famed/renowned ones; مردان here can mean 'heroes' in epic register). خوی + ش together form خویش ('his own') — خوی = kin/possession base and ش = the possessive clitic 'his/her/its'.
segments.s374-g31: گو here is the narrative/quotative particle often used in epic storytelling ('so/then' or 'thus'). گردکش appears as an epithet or name (not matching the simple noun senses listed) and functions as the agent. نیزه اندر نهاد is a verbal phrase: نیزه = 'spear,' اندر = 'into/among' and نهاد = past of نهادن 'placed/put' (here 'set' or 'thrust').
segments.s374-g32: بران here means 'upon/at them' or 'thereon' and functions adverbially/locatively. گردگیران (literally 'dust-cloak wearers') is an epithet for certain fighters (those wrapped in dust-cloaks). یبغو in this context is not the verbal sense 'they transgress' but part of a tribal/title name (Yabghu), so it does not match the listed senses exactly; نژاد = 'lineage/stock' (so together 'the Yabghu-born/of Yabghu lineage').
segments.s374-g17: نگردیم is a negated, first‑person‑plural verb form meaning roughly 'we will not turn/return' or 'we will not allow (someone) to return'; here it functions as a resolved, collective prohibition/determination. The phrase یک تن ازین جنگ باز is the object/goal of that verb: یک تن 'a single person', ازین جنگ 'from this battle' (از + این, here written as separate words in the line above but semantically 'from this'), and باز 'back/again' (adverbial) — together 'a single person to turn back from this fight.' Word order is verb + object phrase (typical poetic inversion).
segments.s374-g18: نداریم is the present/near‑sense 'we do not have / we do not grant' (1st plural), used here in the sense 'we will not give/allow'. The noun phrase زین بدکنان چنگ باز functions as the direct object: زین ('saddle' literally, here part of an idiomatic phrase referring to mounts/means of escape), بدکنان 'the evildoers/malefactors', and چنگ باز 'an open/available grasp' (literally 'open claw' — idiomatically 'escape/mercy'); together: 'we grant these evildoers no open grasp/escape.' The clause expresses a refusal to give the enemy any means of flight; word order places the verb before its compound object for poetic emphasis.
segments.s374-g19: بر اسپان is a prepositional phrase 'on (their) horses' (بر + اسپان). بکردند is the simple past, third‑person plural 'they made/did' (here 'they tightened/fastened'); its object is the implied girths (تنگ) described by the following adjective cluster. تنگ استوار are adjectival/complement phrases 'tight, secure/steady' describing how the girths were made. The clause therefore means 'they drew the girths tight and secure on the horses.'
segments.s374-g20: برفتند is past tense third‑person plural 'they went / they rode off.' یکدل is an adverbial adjective 'one‑hearted / of one mind' (modifying the verb: they went united). سوی کارزار is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the battlefield.' Typical Persian order here puts the verb before its adverbial modifier and directional complement for a brisk, narrative effect.
segments.s374-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' ایشان is the third‑person pronoun 'they.' فگندند is an archaic/poetic past form 'they threw / they cast' (classical f‑ prefix instead of modern انداختند). اسپ is 'horse/steed' as the direct object, and از میان here functions idiomatically as 'into the midst / among' (literally 'from/among the middle' but used to indicate insertion into a crowd). The clause thus reads 'When they cast horses into the midst...' — note the classical verb form and the inverted, compact object phrase.
segments.s374-g22: This is a coordinated noun phrase: گوان و جوانان ایرانیان = 'the گوان and the youths of the Iranians.' گوان here functions as a noun meaning a class of men/warriors (the provided sense list does not capture this nominal sense), و is the conjunction 'and', جوانان is the plural 'young men / youths', and ایرانیان specifies that these groups are Iranians (apposition/genitive). Together they name who were in the midst into which the horses were flung.
segments.s374-g23: همه یکسر is an emphatic adverbial phrase 'all / all at once' (همه 'everyone/all', یکسر used adverbially as 'at once/entirely'). از جای برخاستند is a verb phrase 'rose from their place(s)' (از = 'from', جای = 'place', برخاستند = past 'they rose up'). The order places the adverbial after 'همه' for emphasis: 'All, at once, rose from their places.'
segments.s374-g24: جهان را is the definite object 'the world' (را marking definiteness/emphasis). به جوشن is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with/into armor' (به + جوشن), and بیاراستند is past third‑person plural 'they adorned / they arrayed.' Poetically, this line means they 'adorned the world in armor'—a hyperbolic way of saying they donned armor or made the field look armed. Word order: object + instrumental phrase + verb for rhetorical emphasis.
segments.s374-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بازآورید is a perfective past verb 'brought back' (classical narrative past; subject is not explicit here). آن گرانمایه کین is the definite object phrase 'that precious vengeance' following the verb; گرانمایه is an adjective 'precious/valuable' modifying کین 'vengeance/hatred'.
segments.s374-g2: بر introduces the prepositional phrase 'upon/on' and governs اسپ زریری 'the golden steed' (اسپ = steed). برافگند is a perfective past verb 'threw/placed' and takes زین 'saddle' as its object; زین appears after the verb by poetic inversion ('flung the saddle upon the steed').
segments.s374-g3: خرامید is a past tense verb 'strode proudly' (a vigorous, stately gait). تازان is the subject 'the raiders' (plural) that follows the verb here by poetic word order. به آوردگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the battlefield' indicating destination.
segments.s374-g4: به سه بهره is a measure phrase 'into three shares/parts' (به + numeral + noun). کرد is the past verb 'made/did' used here to mean 'divided (into)'; آن کیانی سپاه is the subject phrase 'that Kayanian host' (subject appears after the verb, a common classical ordering).
segments.s374-g5: ازان سه = 'of those three' (ازان = 'of those / from them'). یکی را is the definite object 'one (of them)' marked by را. به + proper name (بستور) expresses the indirect object 'to Bostur' and داد is the perfective past 'gave' (the doer/subject is not phrased here and appears after).
segments.s374-g6: This phrase supplies the second recipient: دگر 'another' + آن سپهدار فرخ‌نژاد 'that commander Farrokhnezhad'. The verb from the previous segment (داد) is understood to govern this phrase as well (elliptical coordination common in epic narrative).
segments.s374-g7: دگر بهره را 'the other/remaining share' is the definite object (را marks it). بر برادر is the indirect object 'to the brother' (بر + noun). سپرد is the perfective past verb 'entrusted/handed over'; here the verb comes before its explicit subject, which follows in the next segment.
segments.s374-g8: بزرگان ایران و مردان گرد is the subject phrase 'the nobles/great men of Iran and the gathered/assembled men'. گرد here qualifies مردان as 'assembled' or 'a band' (so the whole phrase names the agents who performed the entrusting).

sentences.s374-s21: Then the Iranians fell upon that merciless Chinese host.
sentences.s374-s22: They killed many of them in every direction; no one was spared—what a marvel!
sentences.s374-s17: He continually sewed them — their chests and their backs opened — so that he killed all the rebels.
sentences.s374-s18: When the Khagan learned this — that (enough) remained — no one came forward before him.
sentences.s374-s19: The host stirred and was set in motion, and the affair turned; thus it continued until the day passed.
sentences.s374-s20: At that moment he fell into wrath, and he turned his face toward the wilderness.
sentences.s374-s5: Sim‑Bahre kept the portion to himself, for he had a voice that roared like a cloud.
sentences.s374-s6: When, in that manner, an auspicious and pure‑bodied man appeared, another swordsman brought him to the ground.
sentences.s374-s7: They stood together before him, for it was his evil habit to break an army.
sentences.s374-s8: Hamidun (and others) sealed a pact on this: that if the enemy's sword should strike the ground...
sentences.s374-s13: They killed so many of them that the battlefield's space grew cramped.
sentences.s374-s14: So much blood flowed over hill and plain that even the millstones turned back stained with blood.
sentences.s374-s15: When Arjasp saw that, he came forward before the renowned champions and his own men.
sentences.s374-s16: Then — so the narrator — Gerdkesh set his spear in (thrust forward) among those dust-cloak men of Yabghu's line.
sentences.s374-s9: We will not allow a single man to turn back from this fight; we give these evildoers no free escape (no mercy).
sentences.s374-s10: They tightened the girths on their horses stoutly, and, one‑hearted, went toward the battlefield.
sentences.s374-s11: When they flung their horses into the midst, among the ranks of the men and the youths of the Iranians...
sentences.s374-s12: All at once they rose from their places and arrayed the world in armor.
sentences.s374-s1: When they brought back that precious vengeance, they flung the golden saddle upon the steed.
sentences.s374-s2: The raiders strode proudly to the battlefield; the Kayanian host divided itself into three parts.
sentences.s374-s3: Of those three, one they gave to Bostur, another to the commander Farrokhnezhad.
sentences.s374-s4: The nobles of Iran and the assembled men entrusted the remaining share to the brother.
